hero.sub: Speak naturally in Yoruba, Hausa, Igbo, or English and receive personalised pregnancy, newborn care, nutrition, and vaccination guidance.

features.sub: From pregnancy tracking to newborn care — MamaVoice guides you every step of the way.

voice.title: Voice-First Consultations
voice.desc: Speak naturally in Yoruba, Hausa, Igbo, or English — our AI understands and responds in your language with trusted medical guidance.

specialist.title: Specialist Connect
specialist.desc: Instant access to verified OBGYNs, midwives, and pediatricians across Nigeria for virtual or in-person consultations.

health.title: Health Tracking
health.desc: Monitor blood pressure, weight, baby movements, and key milestones automatically throughout your pregnancy journey.

nutrition.title: Nutrition Guidance
nutrition.desc: Personalized meal plans featuring local Nigerian foods rich in folate, iron, and protein — foods you actually know and love.

vaccine.title: Vaccination Monitor
vaccine.desc: Never miss a prenatal or newborn vaccine. Get smart reminders with a full timeline for mother and baby.

newborn.title: Newborn Care
newborn.desc: Evidence-based guidance for breastfeeding, cord care, diaper rash, fever response, and early childhood milestones.

howItWorks.sub: No complex setup. No data required. Just download, speak, and get the guidance you need.

s1.title: Download & Register
s1.desc: Get MamaVoice on iOS or Android. Register with just your phone number — no email required.

s2.title: Set Your Profile
s2.desc: Enter your due date, language preference, and basic health history. Takes under 2 minutes.

s3.title: Ask by Voice
s3.desc: Tap the mic and speak freely. Ask anything about your pregnancy in your own language.

s4.title: Track Daily Health
s4.desc: Log symptoms, weight, and baby kicks. Get automated reminders for medications and vaccines.

s5.title: Connect with Doctors
s5.desc: Book video or voice consultations with verified specialists from the comfort of your home.

s6.title: Share & Grow
s6.desc: Invite fellow mothers. Share milestones and encouragement in your community group.

voice.sub: MamaVoice understands Yoruba, Hausa, Igbo, and English — responding with trusted medical guidance instantly.
voice.msg1: I am 28 weeks pregnant and my legs are swollen. What should I do?
voice.msg2: Mild swelling during pregnancy is common. Elevate your feet, drink plenty of water, and avoid standing for long periods. If you experience sudden severe swelling or headaches, visit a clinic immediately.
voice.msg3: Thank you. What iron-rich foods can I eat?

dashboard.sub: Track every metric that matters — all beautifully organised in your personal dashboard.
dashboard.tipText: Stay hydrated — drink 8–10 glasses of water daily for optimal fetal development and to ease leg swelling.

nutrition.sub: MamaVoice recommends local Nigerian superfoods packed with nutrients your baby needs to thrive.
nutrition.ctaSub: Get personalised meal plans based on your trimester

moimoi.name: Moi Moi
moimoi.desc: Steamed bean pudding made from blended black-eyed peas — a pregnancy superfood.
moimoi.b1: Rich in folate for neural tube development
moimoi.b2: Iron supports red blood cell production

ugu.name: Ugu Soup
ugu.desc: Fluted pumpkin leaf soup — one of Nigeria's most nutrient-dense vegetables.
ugu.b0: Highest iron content of any Nigerian leaf
ugu.b1: Vitamin A supports fetal eye development
ugu.b2: Calcium and magnesium for bone formation

pap.name: Pap & Groundnut
pap.desc: Fermented corn porridge (akamu) paired with protein-rich groundnuts.
pap.b1: Healthy fats from groundnuts for brain development
pap.b2: High energy — ideal for the third trimester

specialists.sub: Browse trusted nurses, midwives, and doctors. Book virtual consultations right from the app.

webApp.sub: No registration needed. Chat with our AI health assistant instantly. Ask about symptoms, nutrition, vaccinations — anything on your mind.
webApp.footnote: For personalised tracking & vaccinations — download the mobile app

download.sub: Get personalised health tracking, vaccination reminders, offline voice support, and more — available on Android and iPhone.

footer.tagline: Empowering mothers across Africa with trusted voice-first maternal and newborn health guidance.

chat.greetingSub: Your trusted AI health companion. Ask me anything about pregnancy, nutrition, newborn care, or vaccinations.
chat.disclaimer: MamaVoice provides health education, not medical diagnosis. Always consult a doctor for emergencies.
chat.nudge: Get personalised tracking, vaccination reminders & more
chat.p0: I'm 28 weeks pregnant and my legs are swollen. What should I do?
chat.p1: What iron-rich Nigerian foods should I eat in my second trimester?
chat.p2: When is my Tetanus vaccine due during pregnancy?
chat.p3: My newborn won't stop crying at night. Is this normal?
chat.rSwollen: Mild swelling (oedema) is very common in pregnancy, especially in the legs and feet after week 20. Elevate your feet when resting, drink at least 8 glasses of water daily, and avoid standing for long periods. Wear comfortable shoes and sleep on your left side. ⚠️ If you notice sudden severe swelling in your face or hands, or swelling with headaches or blurred vision, please visit a clinic immediately — this could be a sign of preeclampsia.
chat.rIron:
Great question! Iron-rich Nigerian foods you can enjoy safely during pregnancy include:

• Ugu (fluted pumpkin leaves) — one of the richest sources of iron in Nigerian cuisine
• Moi Moi (steamed bean pudding) — packed with iron and protein
• Liver — extremely high in iron (limit to once a week)
• Ofada rice with vegetable stew
• Ogbono soup with leafy greens

Pair these with Vitamin C foods (like oranges or tomatoes) to help your body absorb the iron better.
chat.rVaccine:
The standard vaccination schedule for pregnant women in Nigeria includes:

💉 Tetanus Toxoid 1 (TT1) — given at first antenatal visit
💉 Tetanus Toxoid 2 (TT2) — given 4 weeks after TT1
💉 TT Booster — if you had prior tetanus vaccinations

You may also receive the Influenza vaccine, especially during flu season. Always confirm your schedule with your healthcare provider at every antenatal visit.
chat.rCry:
Newborn crying is completely normal and is their only way to communicate! Common reasons include:

• Hunger (most common — try feeding)
• Dirty diaper
• Needs to be held/comforted
• Overtired or overstimulated
• Gas or tummy discomfort (try gentle tummy massages)

Most babies cry 1–3 hours per day in the first few weeks. If your baby is inconsolable for over 3 hours, has a fever above 38°C, or isn't feeding well, please see a doctor right away.
chat.rPain:
Back pain during pregnancy is very common. Try these safe remedies:

• Gentle prenatal yoga or stretching
• Sleep on your left side with a pillow between your knees
• Wear supportive, flat shoes
• Apply a warm (not hot) compress to the painful area

If the pain is severe or accompanied by fever or urinary symptoms, please consult your doctor.
chat.rNausea:
Morning sickness is very common in the first trimester. Here's what can help:

• Eat small, frequent meals — don't let your stomach get empty
• Try dry crackers or akamu (pap) first thing in the morning
• Sip cold water or ginger tea slowly
• Avoid spicy, fatty, or strongly-smelling foods

If you're vomiting so much you can't keep food or water down, please see a doctor — you may need IV fluids.
chat.rDefault:
That's a great question. MamaVoice is here to support you every step of your pregnancy and newborn journey. For the most accurate guidance on your specific situation, I recommend:

1. Speaking with one of our verified specialists in the app
2. Attending your next antenatal clinic visit
3. Calling your nearest health facility if you have urgent concerns

Is there anything specific about pregnancy, nutrition, vaccinations, or newborn care I can help you with today?
chat.webPreviewBanner: You're using the web preview. Get the full app for the best experience.
chat.sttLangFallback: Voice input may not work for this language on your device. Please type your message instead.
